glossary.definition: Deals with developing hardware and software solutions endowed with human-similar intellectual processes.
glossary.sentence: Artificial intelligence deals with developing hardware and software solutions endowed with human-similar intellectual processes, such as the ability to reason, discover meaning, generalise, or learn from experience (Copeland, 2020).

glossary.definition: A general concept encompassing various manifestations of intelligent performance across different entities like humans, animals, and plants.
glossary.sentence: In this topic, you will find out what we understand by intelligence, how artificial intelligence is defined, and what is a fundamental approach to assessment of intelligence of a computer system.

glossary.definition: The ability studied in-depth across various scientific fields like philosophy, linguistics, neuroscience, with psychology offering theories about its nature and characteristics.
glossary.sentence: It is problematic to measure intelligence due to the diversity of people, which is determined by, for example, linguistic, cultural, physical condition and other differences (Legg and Hutter, 2007) .

glossary.definition: Primarily related to learning abilities observed in animals like monkeys, dolphins, elephants, rats, or crows.
glossary.sentence: It is mostly related to learning ability of such animals as monkeys, dolphins, elephant, rat, or crows.

glossary.definition: Intellectual performance exhibited by plants, not based on a brain and nervous system.
glossary.sentence: It is currently known that plant intelligence is not based on a brain and nervous system (Parise, Gagliano and Souza, 2020) .

glossary.definition: A definition that encompasses the essence of different types of intelligence, not limited to any particular set of senses or environments.
glossary.sentence: Thus, in order to cover the diversity of the existent manifestations of intelligent performance, a general definition of intelligence should (Legg and Hutter, 2007) : encompass the essence of the different types of intelligence; not be limited to any particular set of senses, environments, goals, and any specific kind of intelligence implementation structures;

glossary.term: Artificial Intelligence Definition by John McCarthy
glossary.definition: The study of simulating aspects of learning or other features of intelligence in machines.
glossary.sentence: The term “artificial intelligence” was chosen as neutral enough to avoid highlighting a particular topic of the summer school. The definition proposed by McCarthy defines artificial intelligence by emphasizing its similarity to human intelligence, and so it is the following: “The study is to proceed on the basis of the conjecture that every aspect of learning or any other feature of intelligence can in principle be so precisely described that a machine can be made to simulate it.

glossary.term: Categories of AI Definitions (Russell and Norvig)
glossary.definition: Four categories grouping definitions based on thought processes, reasoning, behavior, human intelligence, or ideal rationality as standards.
glossary.sentence: Stuart Russell and Peter Norvig (2009) have grouped definitions of artificial intelligence into four categories.

glossary.definition: An approach underlying systems that think humanly, focusing on information representation and processing in the human mind and computers.
glossary.sentence: A cognitive modelling approach underlies systems that think humanly. It considers human being to be an information processing system.

glossary.definition: An approach underlying systems that think rationally, representing knowledge as logical rules for inference.
glossary.sentence: Systems that think rationally are based on logics . As a result, knowledge is represented as logical rules, for example, if we know that “All students have a student ID” and “Alex is a student”, than we can conclude (to infer new knowledge) that “Alex has a student ID”.

glossary.definition: An approach underlying systems that act humanly.
glossary.sentence: The approach of the Turing test underlies systems that act humanly.

glossary.definition: Systems developed to support humans in completing various functions and activities or coping with different types of problems.
glossary.sentence: At the beginning of the study course, you learnt out that intelligent computer systems are being developed to support humans in the completion of various functions and activities, or, in other words, to cope with different types of problems.

glossary.definition: Systems acting rationally based on the approach of rational agents.
glossary.sentence: Systems acting rationally are based on the approach of rational agents .

glossary.definition: Operates in a changing environment and needs to find the best sequence of actions that maximise the end goal.
glossary.sentence: Reinforcement learning is suitable for agents (like robots, self-driving cars, or a game- playing software) who operate in a changing environment and, to act in the environment, they need to find the best sequence of actions that maximise the end goal (Pant, 2019).

glossary.definition: One that acts to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.
glossary.sentence: A rational agent is one that acts to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.

glossary.definition: One of the fundamental tests in the field of artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence.
glossary.sentence: The Turing test underlies systems that act humanly and it is also one of the fundamental tests in the field of artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence.

glossary.definition: Capability for maintaining successful communication with the tester as the human being does.
glossary.sentence: natural language processing for maintaining successful communication with the tester as the human being does;

glossary.definition: the theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems to support intelligent performance.
glossary.sentence: In general, “ knowledge representation is the theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems” (Jones, 2009). It can be defined more precisely as an incorporation of full range formally described knowledge into an intelligent system with the aim of supporting intelligent performance of the system (Graham and Barrett, 1997).

glossary.definition: Memory used by humans to store all previously acquired knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Humans use for this purpose long-term memory to store all previously acquired knowledge

glossary.definition: Memory used by humans to store knowledge processed at a specific time.
glossary.sentence: short-term memory - to store knowledge processed at a specific time;

glossary.definition: Capability in order to use the stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions.
glossary.sentence: automated reasoning, in order to use the stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions;

glossary.definition: A method of data analysis that automates analytical model building and is a branch of artificial intelligence based on the idea that systems can learn from data, identify patterns and make decisions with minimal human intervention.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning is a method of data analysis that automates analytical model building. It is a branch of artificial intelligence based on the idea that systems can learn from data, identify patterns and make decisions with minimal human intervention.

glossary.definition: Excludes direct physical interaction between the computer and the tester.
glossary.sentence: The regular Turing test excludes direct physical interaction between the computer and the tester, because it is not necessary to simulate a human being to make conclusions about intelligence.

glossary.definition: Brings forward two more requirements: the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.
glossary.sentence: However, the so called the Total Turing test brings forward two more requirements (Russell and Norvig, 2009) :

glossary.definition: Capability to perceive objects.
glossary.sentence: the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: Capability to manipulate objects and move about.
glossary.sentence: the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: Related to understanding of those principles which make possible intelligent performance both in natural and artificial systems.
glossary.sentence: scientific goal is related to understanding of those principles which make possible intelligent performance both in natural and artificial systems.

glossary.definition: Related to the development of useful intelligent artefacts.
glossary.sentence: engineering goal is related to the development of useful intelligent artefacts.

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems
glossary.sentence: Connectionist artificial intelligence Symbolic artificial intelligence An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons) An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons)
glossary.sentence: Connectionist artificial intelligence Symbolic artificial intelligence An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons) An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems

glossary.definition: not enough to simulate human intelligence because intelligence is genetically biological and cannot be fixed by symbols.
glossary.sentence: Critics claim that the hypothesis is not enough to simulate human intelligence because intelligence is genetically biological and cannot be fixed by symbols (Luger, 2009) .

glossary.definition: a system of physical symbols capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour.
glossary.sentence: A physical system is capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols (Luger, 2009) .

glossary.definition: correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality.
glossary.sentence: A physical symbol system includes (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009; Poole and Mackworth, 2010) : symbols that correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality

glossary.definition: consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way. Symbolic structures thus depict important aspects of a problem domain.
glossary.sentence: symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way. Symbolic structures thus depict important aspects of a problem domain;

glossary.definition: (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.
glossary.sentence: operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures; moreover, the operations themselves can be represented by symbols and symbolic structures in the system.

glossary.definition: derive from artificial neural network models.
glossary.sentence: These paradigms correspond to two dominant models of information processing in contemporary cognitive science (Bermúdez, 2014) : the first is related to the physical symbol system hypothesis, while the second derives from artificial neural network models.

glossary.definition: essentially involves transforming physical symbols according to rules.
glossary.sentence: Summarising all of the above, it can be said that " all intelligent behavior essentially involves transforming physical symbols according to rules " (Bermúdez, 2014) .

glossary.definition: knowledge (objects and their relations) through a set of symbols.
glossary.sentence: Intelligent computer systems based on this paradigm therefore explicitly represent problem domain knowledge (objects and their relations) through a set of symbols (Luger, 2009 ).

glossary.definition: a famous example of criticism. Searle describes a situation in which the physical system correctly manipulates physical symbols, but the system has no intelligence.
glossary.sentence: John Searle’s Chinese room argument is a famous example of criticism. Searle describes a situation in which the physical system correctly manipulates physical symbols, but the system has no intelligence.

glossary.definition: underlies almost all practical and theoretical research in expert systems, search, planning systems and natural language processing (Luger, 2009) .
glossary.sentence: Despite criticisms, the physical symbol system hypothesis underlies almost all practical and theoretical research in expert systems, search, planning systems and natural language processing (Luger, 2009) .

glossary.definition: Biologically motivated computing structures that are conceptually modeled after the brain.
glossary.sentence: According to (Jones, 2009), “neural networks are biologically motivated computing structures that are conceptually modeled after the brain.”

glossary.definition: connected processing units that mimic the neurons of the human brain.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks are simplified models of the human brain consisting of a large number of connected processing units (artificial neurons) that mimic the neurons of the human brain, and numerical weights that are attached to the connections between the units, reflecting the strength of these connections.

glossary.definition: weights of the connections, which are changed during the operation of the network.
glossary.sentence: As a result, in connectionist artificial intelligence, knowledge is encoded as numerical values in the weights of the connections, which are changed during the operation of the network.

glossary.definition: recognise patterns in the data and reflect them in their structure.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge is thus implicitly reflected in the organisation and interaction of artificial neurons, and instead of searching for possible generalisations, connectionist models recognise patterns in the data and reflect them in their structure (Luger, 2009) .

glossary.definition: crucial in generating intellectual behaviour.
glossary.sentence: Symbolic artificial intelligence considers the use of knowledge in reasoning and learning to be crucial in generating intellectual behaviour, whereas connectionist artificial intelligence attributes this to the discovery of relationships in data (with little or no prior knowledge) (Goel, 2021) .

glossary.definition: complements artificial neural networks in the AlphaGo program.
glossary.sentence: It is important to note that the connectionist and symbolic artificial intelligence paradigms of thinking in contemporary artificial intelligence are complementary rather than competing (Russell and Norvig, 2020 ), for example, tree-based search complements artificial neural networks in the AlphaGo program, which is designed to play the board game Go (Goel, 2021).

glossary.definition: designed to play the board game Go.
glossary.sentence: It is important to note that the connectionist and symbolic artificial intelligence paradigms of thinking in contemporary artificial intelligence are complementary rather than competing (Russell and Norvig, 2020 ), for example, tree-based search complements artificial neural networks in the AlphaGo program, which is designed to play the board game Go (Goel, 2021).

glossary.definition: Proves to be learnable
glossary.sentence: Connectionist representation of knowledge proves to be learnable Symbolic representation of knowledge is not much learnable Connectionist artificial intelligence solves the problem but is not able to effectively reason the solution Symbolic artificial intelligence have a very good sense of reasoning for the solved problem

glossary.definition: Is not much learnable

glossary.definition: Based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc.
glossary.sentence: There are several approaches to artificial intelligence , based on comparing the capabilities of artificial intelligence systems with those of humans. Narrow (weak) artificial intelligence , that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc.

glossary.definition: An approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated. It is related to the development of such computer systems that are capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions.
glossary.sentence: Artificial general intelligence or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated . It is related to the development of such computer systems that are capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions.

glossary.definition: An intelligence that surpasses human capabilities. It is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills.
glossary.sentence: Artificial super intelligence is an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities  and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills.

glossary.definition: Focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales.
glossary.sentence: Additionally, to the previously mentioned approaches there is also the so called new/nouvelle artificial intelligence . “It focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales”.

glossary.definition: A paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents.
glossary.sentence: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents.

glossary.definition: Studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food.
glossary.sentence: It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish.

glossary.definition: Studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish.
glossary.sentence: It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish.

glossary.definition: do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning.
glossary.sentence: In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning, and strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.

glossary.definition: based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.
glossary.sentence: In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning, and strong methods, based on the great amount of knowledge about the problem under consideration.

glossary.term: zero-sum deterministic two-person games with perfect information
glossary.definition: A type of game characterized by two players competing against each other, making alternate moves, having complete information about the course of the game, no randomness factors involved, and one player’s loss being equal to another player’s gain.
glossary.sentence: zero-sum deterministic two-player games with perfect information. The following aspects characterise this type of game: two players participate in the game and compete against each other;

glossary.term: deterministic two-player games of perfect information
glossary.definition: Games such as checkers, tic-tac-toe and chess, where both players have all the information about the course of the game and random factors are not involved.
glossary.sentence: Deterministic two-player games of perfect information, such as checkers, tic-tac-toe and chess, in which both players have all the information about the course of the game (for example, which objects or figures are still available, which moves have been performed and which next moves are possible), and random factors are not involved.

glossary.term: stochastic two-player games of perfect information
glossary.definition: Games such as backgammon, where both players are aware of what is happening on the playing board but a die is used, meaning that the players cannot make a precise prediction about the score on the die in the next move.
glossary.sentence: Stochastic two-player games of perfect information, such as backgammon, in which both players still are aware of what is happening on the playing board but a die is used, meaning that the players cannot make a precise prediction about the score on the die in the next move.

glossary.term: stochastic multi-player games of imperfect information
glossary.definition: Games such as bridge and poker, where part of the information about the course of the game is hidden from the players (i. e. the players can see only their own cards) and where a die could also be used to introduce randomness.
glossary.sentence: Stochastic multi-player games of imperfect information, such as bridge and poker, in which part of the information about the course of the game is hidden from the players (i. e. the players can see only their own cards) and where a die could also be used to introduce randomness.

glossary.definition: A classification criterion for games based on the availability of information about the course of the game for both players, meaning that all relevant information is known to both players.

glossary.definition: A classification criterion for games based on the availability of information about the course of the game for both players, meaning that some relevant information is hidden from one or more players.

glossary.definition: A classification criterion for games based on the level of randomness associated with a game, meaning that there are no random factors involved.
