glossary.sentence: From the given data of a problem toward a goal. This is called data-driven search or forward chaining . The search process starts from a root node and it proceeds in forward direction in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: The search process starts from a leaf node and it proceeds in backward direction in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: From a goal back to the data. This is called goal-driven search or backward chaining . The search process starts from a leaf node and it proceeds in backward direction in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: a system of roads between two cities in France: Paris and Toulouse that can be used to demonstrate the difference between search algorithms.
glossary.sentence: Imagine that we are building a recommendation system that will advise tourists about the selection of roads between two cities in France: Paris and Toulouse. In Figure 2, you can see some of the roads between the cities that can be used to demonstrate the difference between search algorithms.

glossary.definition: a system that will advise tourists about the selection of roads between two cities in France: Paris and Toulouse.
glossary.sentence: Imagine that we are building a recommendation system that will advise tourists about the selection of roads between two cities in France: Paris and Toulouse. In Figure 2, you can see some of the roads between the cities that can be used to demonstrate the difference between search algorithms.

glossary.definition: a type of search algorithm that processes only nodes and arcs of the state space graph without additional information about the quality of the nodes.
glossary.sentence: In the case of uninformed search, all of the paths represented in the state space graph are identical, because uninformed search algorithms process only nodes and arcs of the state space graph. They do not have additional information about the quality of the nodes.

glossary.definition: a type of search algorithm that can take into account various factors such as distance, location, road surface, number of traffic lights, etc., represented as numerical values showing the quality of each node in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic search algorithms are able to distinguish between paths represented in the state space graph. Therefore, each path differs from all of the others. This is because heuristic search can take into account such information as the distance between any two settlements, the location of each settlement (north, west, etc.) relative to Paris, the surface of the road leading to each settlement, the number of traffic lights between any two settlements, and many other factors.

glossary.definition: A list that contains nodes to be evaluated in the current iteration. It initially contains only the search start node and is updated with the best nodes according to the beam width.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm always simultaneously expands all nodes located in the OPEN list at each iteration,

glossary.definition: A list that contains nodes that have been evaluated or generated but are not among the best nodes selected according to the beam width.
glossary.sentence: Other nodes are placed on the CLOSED list.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm searches the state space graph using a depth-first search (Ertel, 2011)

glossary.definition: a type of data structure where new nodes are added to the left side of the OPEN list, working according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a stack. A stack works according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 8).

glossary.definition: A terminal node in the game tree where the outcome of the game (win or loss) is determined.
glossary.sentence: It reaches a leaf node in which the minimiser has the highest score and therefore wins the game.

glossary.definition: The fifth step in the algorithm's process where the current node I is expanded by generating its three descendants - nodes J, K, and G.
glossary.sentence: Iteration 5: The algorithm expands the current node I by generating its three descendants - nodes J, K, and G. Taking into account that the node J has been already added to the CLOSED list, it is not added to the OPEN list again. The nodes K and G are added to the left side of the OPEN list as new nodes in the sequence that they were found.

glossary.definition: The path with the smallest number of arcs to the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: This algorithm is also guaranteed to find the shortest path to the search goal node if such a path exists in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: Paths in the state space graph that extend many levels deep, causing Depth-First Search to take a long time to return to higher levels.
glossary.sentence: It means that in a situation where the state space graph has many node levels, Depth-First Search will search for the search goal node for a long time in depth before it returns to higher levels.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that overcomes a problem of going along indefinitely deep paths but may not find a solution path if the search goal node is located below the depth limit.
glossary.sentence: Depth-Limited Search overcomes a problem of going along indefinitely deep paths. At the same time, it is not able to find a solution path if the search goal node is located below the depth limit.

glossary.definition: A guideline for Depth-Limited Search on how deep the algorithm can go in searching.
glossary.sentence: However, one of the main problems of Depth-Limited Search is a lack of guidelines on how to choose the depth limit for a particular problem.

glossary.definition: Nodes considered as terminal points in Depth-Limited Search, depending on the search direction and the defined depth limit.
glossary.sentence: Nodes located at the depth limit are considered to be leaf/root nodes (depending on the search direction).

glossary.definition: Labels assigned to nodes indicating their level within the state space graph, used to determine if descendants or ancestors can be generated.
glossary.sentence: Depth labels allow the algorithm "to understand" if it is possible to generate descendants/ancestors of the current node or if the current node should be considered a leaf/root node.

glossary.definition: The node being evaluated in the current iteration. It is taken from the OPEN list and expanded to generate its descendants.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm takes the node located on the first position in the OPEN list—the node A—and it becomes the current node.

glossary.definition: A node that is moved to the CLOSED list as an already expanded node.
glossary.sentence: The node E is moved to the CLOSED list as an already expanded node.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm that explores closer branches and then goes to the ends of branches, which consumes significant computational resources.
glossary.sentence: The difference between Breadth-First Search and Bidirectional Search is evident if we use the analogy of the state space graph and tree crown (Figure 21).

glossary.definition: An analogy used to represent the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Using the same analogy of the state space graph and tree crown (Figure 15), we can see that this algorithm explores closer branches and then goes to the ends of branches.

glossary.definition: Nodes generated from or leading to the current node, depending on the search direction.
glossary.sentence: evaluates their descendants/ancestors (depending on a search direction),

glossary.definition: A type of data structure where new nodes are added to the right side and processed from the left side.
glossary.sentence: In the implementation of this algorithm, new nodes are added to the right side of the OPEN list. Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a queue.

glossary.definition: A principle where the first data element entered is the first to be processed.
glossary.sentence: A queue works according to the “First in, First Out” principle: the first data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 18).

glossary.definition: An algorithm that performs a search simultaneously in both directions - data-driven search and goal-driven search - until it finds a point of contact in both directions.
glossary.sentence: The difference between Breadth-First Search and Bidirectional Search is evident if we use the analogy of the state space graph and tree crown (Figure 21).

glossary.definition: Layers or stages in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The reason is that when searching in a level-by-level fashion, the shortest path will be found sooner because it consists of a smaller number of arcs, which means that the search goal node is located at higher levels.

glossary.definition: Lists used in Bidirectional Search to keep track of nodes that need to be expanded, with two separate lists for data-driven search and goal-driven search.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm uses two OPEN lists and two CLOSED lists. The lists are filled in simultaneously in both directions.

glossary.definition: Lists used in Bidirectional Search to keep track of nodes that have already been expanded, with two separate lists for data-driven search and goal-driven search.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm uses two OPEN lists and two CLOSED lists. The lists are filled in simultaneously in both directions.

glossary.definition: Nodes directly connected to a given node that are generated during the expansion of that node in Bidirectional Search.
glossary.sentence: The descendants of the node A are the nodes D, H, and E. None of them have been added to the OPEN list or to the CLOSED list of data-driven search.

glossary.definition: Nodes directly connected to a given node that are considered during the expansion of that node in Bidirectional Search.
glossary.sentence: The ancestors of the node K are the nodes I, E, F, and G. None of them have been added to the OPEN list or to the CLOSED list of goal-driven search.

glossary.definition: Problem-specific knowledge often based on experience, used to solve problems. It is inherently individual and varies among individuals due to different experiences.
glossary.sentence: humans also have a lot of heuristic knowledge related to routine (daily) situations

glossary.definition: Strategies used to find a path from the initial state to the goal state in an effective way.
glossary.sentence: Taking into account that a problem can have a large number of solutions, people use strategies (heuristic methods) to find a path from the initial state to the goal state in an effective way (Eysenck & Keane, 2000).

glossary.definition: A function used to find the most promising moves in a partial state space graph.
glossary.sentence: They can be based on the principles of either the Minimax or Alpha-beta pruning, and use a heuristic evaluation function to find the most promising moves.

glossary.definition: Algorithms that receive the search start node and the search goal node as input data and provide a solution path as their output.
glossary.sentence: In Module 2 of our course, we considered the operational steps of uninformed search algorithms. They receive the search start node and the search goal node as input data, the state space graph, a data structure, is used to search for a problem solution, and they provide a solution path as their output.

glossary.definition: Algorithms that receive an additional input - the principles of calculation of the heuristic measures of nodes or, in other words, a heuristic evaluation function.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic search algorithms differ both in the inputs that they need and the operational steps they perform (Figure 2). Heuristic search algorithms receive an additional input - the principles of calculation of the heuristic measures of nodes or, in other words, a heuristic evaluation function.

glossary.definition: The mental effort required for in-depth analysis of a problem.
glossary.sentence: It reduces the cognitive workload that would be needed for the in-depth analysis of a problem

glossary.definition: Values assigned to nodes in a graph by an intelligent computer system to select promising moves during game play.
glossary.sentence: Assigning heuristic measures In both cases, the intelligent computer system assigns heuristic measures to the nodes of the available graph, and these allow the intelligent computer system to select promising moves to make during game play.

glossary.definition: The distance between the search start node and node n, allowing the algorithm to choose shorter paths.
glossary.sentence: f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration; g(n) – the distance between the search start node and node n; this value allows the algorithm to choose the shorter paths that are available;

glossary.definition: A heuristic measure characterizing the quality of node n, estimating the distance between the node n and the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration; g(n) – the distance between the search start node and node n; this value allows the algorithm to choose the shorter paths that are available; h(n) – a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node;

glossary.definition: A game used as an example to demonstrate the idea of problem-specific factors and their numerical representation in an intelligent computer system.
glossary.sentence: Imagine we are building an intelligent computer system that will play a game of Tic-tac-toe.

glossary.definition: A game where players move tiles onto an empty square to achieve a goal state, used as an example to illustrate heuristic evaluation.
glossary.sentence: Another example is the game of 8-puzzle. There are 8 numbered tiles and 9 squares. Players are allowed to move a tile onto an empty square with the aim of achieving the goal state that you can see in Figure 5.

glossary.definition: A measure used to evaluate nodes in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Applying the defined factor, the first and the third node will acquire a value of 5, while the second node will get a value of 3

glossary.definition: The total distance of tiles from their intended places.
glossary.sentence: Another factor that can be used to evaluate nodes is to sum up distances of tiles from their intended places.

glossary.definition: Pairs of tiles that need to be swapped to reach the correct configuration.
glossary.sentence: the number of pairs of tile reversals (Figure 8). This means that we need to change the places of two tiles located close one to another, and usually, it takes more than two moves to put them back.

glossary.definition: Real-valued weights expressing the strength or importance of each neuron input in terms of its influence on the output.
glossary.sentence: a set of real-valued weights (one for each input) that express the strength, or, in other words, the importance of each neuron input in terms of its influence on the output.

glossary.definition: The average number of arcs per node in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: B – the branching factor of the state space graph . It is the average number of arcs per node.

glossary.definition: The length of the longest path in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: L – depth of the state space graph (usually the length of the longest path).

glossary.definition: The complete count of nodes within a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the total number of nodes (T) is

glossary.definition: A significant problem with N-ply look-ahead where the depth of the look-ahead may not detect a heuristically promising path, leading to a bad situation later in the game.
glossary.sentence: One of the most significant problems with this approach is called the horizon effect (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: An algorithm that always moves to the best node in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The idea of the Hill Climbing algorithm is to always move to the best node in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A feature that allows reduction of the number of states in the game tree by considering only unique states.
glossary.sentence: One of the suggestions is to use a symmetry feature of states, which can be done for the game of Tic-tac-toe (Figure 12). At the first level of the game tree, it is possible to consider only three states: corner, centre, and side, because, for example, it does not matter in which corner the sign is placed.

glossary.definition: A rapid increase in the number of nodes to visit when doubling the depth.
glossary.sentence: However, by doubling the depth (12), the number of nodes to visit is 10 times larger (8190 nodes). So, there is exponential growth.

glossary.definition: A technique not implemented by the Hill Climbing algorithm.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm does not implement backtracking.

glossary.definition: A process that checks if it is the search goal node and stops its operation if true.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm checks if it is the search goal node. It is the search goal node; thus, the algorithm stops its operation.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that always selects the best node based on heuristic measures of nodes.
glossary.sentence: It should be noted that Hill Climbing always selects the best node.

glossary.definition: A node determined by the heuristic measures of nodes.
glossary.sentence: The best node is determined by the heuristic measures of nodes.

glossary.definition: A value assigned to nodes in the algorithm's evaluation process, which can be intermediate or precise.
glossary.sentence: The assigned heuristic measure indicates that in the worst case, the maximiser will have the measure of 1, but in the best case, the measure may be higher since the task of the maximiser is to win the game.

glossary.definition: A shortcoming of Hill Climbing where the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node with a better heuristic measure than its descendants/ancestors.
glossary.sentence: the local maximum problem: the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction)

glossary.definition: A shortcoming of Hill Climbing where the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: the plateau problem: the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that selects the best node found so far and keeps all previously found nodes.
glossary.sentence: The Best-First Search algorithm does not have these shortcomings. Let’s consider it.

glossary.definition: A data structure used to implement the OPEN list in Best-First Search, where the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.
glossary.sentence: The OPEN list is implemented as a data structure called a priority queue (Figure 7). The priority queue works according to the principle that the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that simultaneously expands all nodes located in the OPEN list at each iteration, evaluates their descendants/ancestors (depending on a search direction), and chooses the best nodes from the generated nodes according to the beam width.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm always simultaneously expands all nodes located in the OPEN list at each iteration, evaluates their descendants/ancestors (depending on a search direction), and chooses the best nodes from the generated nodes according to the beam width.

glossary.definition: The number of nodes kept in the OPEN list at each iteration. It determines how many best nodes are selected based on heuristic measures.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the OPEN list always contains the same number of nodes as the beam width,

glossary.definition: A graph where all possible states of the game are represented, allowing for a complete analysis of game outcomes.
glossary.sentence: In the case of the complete state space graph, we can use the following values in the simplest case.

glossary.definition: A more complex case since the results of the game cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.
glossary.sentence: A partial state space graph is a more complex case, since the results of the game cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.

glossary.definition: An entity whose task is to win the game by achieving higher heuristic measures.
glossary.sentence: The assigned heuristic measure indicates that in the worst case, the maximiser will have a measure of 1, but in the best case, the measure may be higher, since the task of the maximiser is to win the game.

glossary.definition: An entity whose task is to minimize the game result for the maximiser by achieving lower heuristic measures.
glossary.sentence: The assigned heuristic measure indicates that in the worst case the minimiser will take the measure of −1, but in the best case, the measure will be lower, since the task of the minimiser is to minimise the game result for the maximiser, i.e. to prevent the maximiser from winning the game.

glossary.definition: Nodes at the specified depth in a partial state space graph, where a game result cannot be identified.
glossary.sentence: the terminal nodes of the sub-graph at the specified depth,

glossary.definition: The outcome of a game determined by evaluating heuristic measures and optimal moves.
glossary.sentence: This is a game result, and we can see that it is the same as provided by the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: Sequences of moves leading to a favorable outcome for a player.
glossary.sentence: Using the game results, winning paths can be identified (Figure 9). These are the same as for the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: An algorithm used to determine the optimal move for a player, considering the opponent's best possible response.
glossary.sentence: This is a game result, and we can see that it is the same as provided by the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: A principle that can be used as a basis for N-ply look-ahead and iterative deepening.
glossary.sentence: They can be based on the principles of either the Minimax or Alpha-beta pruning,

glossary.definition: A value assigned to nodes at MAX levels in alpha-beta pruning, representing the best move to maximize the game result.
glossary.sentence: An alpha number (α) is assigned to nodes at MAX levels. This value should not decrease, since this would represent a bad move. Hence, this number defines the best move that can be made to maximise the result of the game;

glossary.definition: A value assigned to nodes at MIN levels in alpha-beta pruning, representing the best move to minimize the game result.
glossary.sentence: A beta number (β) is assigned to nodes at MIN levels. It should not increase, since this would represent a bad move. This number therefore defines the best move that can be done to minimise the result of the game.

glossary.definition: A pruning technique performed when the β value is smaller than α, cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the β value) is −1, and the measure of the ancestor (the α value) is 1. By comparing the measures, the algorithm finds that the value of β is smaller than α, meaning that an α-cut-off should be performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: A pruning technique performed when the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor, cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the α value) is 1 and the measure of the ancestor (the β value) is 1. By comparing the measures, the algorithm finds that the two nodes have only one common value of 1 or, in another words, the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor, meaning that a β-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: An approach used to process a partial state space graph that explores a limited number of levels denoted by n.
glossary.sentence: The two approaches of N-ply look-ahead and iterative deepening are used to process a partial state space graph (Figure 11).

glossary.definition: An approach that explores the partial state space graph in an iterative manner – at each iteration, it goes one level deeper.
glossary.sentence: Iterative deepening can be used when there is a time limit on making the next move in the game.

glossary.definition: Branches in the game tree that have not yet been evaluated by the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm cuts off one unconsidered path.

glossary.definition: A definitive value assigned to a node based on the evaluation of its descendants or direct outcomes.
glossary.sentence: The node whose unconsidered paths were cut off acquires a precise heuristic measure based on the heuristic measure of the only descendant evaluated so far.

glossary.definition: A preliminary value assigned to a node when it is not possible to determine a precise heuristic measure due to unconsidered descendants or incomplete information.
glossary.sentence: It is not possible to assign a precise heuristic measure in this case, and the algorithm is able to assign only an intermediate heuristic measure of >=1

glossary.definition: A level in the game tree where the maximiser aims to achieve higher heuristic measures.
glossary.sentence: The node is located at the MAX level and the only known heuristic measure is 1.

glossary.definition: A level in the game tree where the minimiser aims to achieve lower heuristic measures.
glossary.sentence: The current node is located at the MIN level, but there is no difference between the heuristic measures of the descendants.

glossary.definition: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node during the algorithm's evaluation process.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the α value) is 1 and the measure of the ancestor (the β value) is 1.

glossary.definition: The heuristic measure of the ancestor node during the algorithm's evaluation process.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the α value) is 1 and the measure of the ancestor (the β value) is 1.

glossary.definition: A representation of scores or values in a specific format, such as “2-3” and the score is 3:6.
glossary.sentence: It reaches an intermediate state where the numerical string is “2-3” and the score is 3:6.

glossary.definition: A node higher up in the game tree hierarchy relative to the current node being evaluated.
glossary.sentence: When the algorithm has assigned an intermediate heuristic measure, it checks whether the ancestor of the current node in the current path also has an intermediate heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: A node lower down in the game tree hierarchy relative to the current node being evaluated.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm goes up one level, via the same path that was used to go down. It finds that this node does not have other unconsidered descendants

glossary.definition: A principle that can be used as a basis for N-ply look-ahead and iterative deepening.
glossary.sentence: They can be based on the principles of either the Minimax or Alpha-beta pruning,

glossary.definition: The study of computers and computing, including their theoretical and algorithmic foundations, hardware and software, and their uses for processing information.
glossary.sentence: Computer science is the broadest concept in this context. It is “the study of computers and computing, including their theoretical and algorithmic foundations, hardware and software, and their uses for processing information” (Tucker & Belford, 2019).

glossary.definition: Explicit learning where a teacher teaches a child. Based on training data with feature values and labels representing the desired solution.
glossary.sentence: Type of learning Explicit (the teacher teaches a child) Implicit (a child is self-sufficient in learning)

glossary.definition: Implicit learning where a child is self-sufficient. Based on unlabelled data to reveal hidden patterns/structure.
glossary.sentence: Type of learning Explicit (the teacher teaches a child) Implicit (a child is self-sufficient in learning)

glossary.definition: Reward-based learning where an agent learns to act in an environment to maximize an end goal.
glossary.sentence: Reinforcement learning is reward-based learning. According to (Atul, 2019), it can be characterised as "My life-my rules!"

glossary.definition: Algorithms that are explicitly programmed by their developers with each step coded in a precise way together with possible inputs and outputs.
glossary.sentence: The conventional algorithms of computer science are explicitly programmed by their developers. It means that the developers code each step of an algorithm in a precise way together with possible inputs and outputs.

glossary.definition: Algorithms that are not based on a precise set of instructions.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning algorithms are not based on a precise set of instructions.

glossary.definition: a representation that should be used and set initial configuration variables (hyperparameters) of the model.
glossary.sentence: the developers provide only general guidelines about a mathematical model that should be used and set initial configuration variables (hyperparameters) of the model

glossary.definition: Finds a category/class to which a new data object can be attributed.
glossary.sentence: The machine learning algorithm finds a category/class to which a new data object can be attributed.

glossary.definition: an internal configuration variable of the model whose value is found (learnt) by a machine learning algorithm during the training.
glossary.sentence: A paramete r (also called a model parameter) - an internal configuration variable of the model whose value is found (learnt) by a machine learning algorithm during the training

glossary.definition: an external configuration variable whose value is typically set by the developer arbitrarily before the training.
glossary.sentence: A hyperparameter (also called a model hyperparameter) - an external configuration variable whose value is typically set by the developer arbitrarily before the training (for example, learning rate, number of neurons in an artificial neural network, etc.)

glossary.definition: a particular unit of data typically represented by a number of feature values.
glossary.sentence: A data object (also called a data point, an observation, an example) - a particular unit of data typically represented by a number of feature values, for example, a data object can represent one specific patient through the values of patient’s temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, etc.

glossary.definition: a collection of data objects.
glossary.sentence: A dataset - a collection of data objects, for example, a set of data about patients who attended Hospital A between January and May in 2019.

glossary.definition: a measurable characteristic/property of a data object used as an input variable to a machine learning algorithm.
glossary.sentence: A feature (also called an attribute) - a measurable characteristic/property of a data object used as an input variable to a machine learning algorithm, for example, the patient’s temperature.

glossary.definition: a specific value of a feature.
glossary.sentence: A feature value – a specific value of a feature, for example, if the feature is “the patient’s temperature”, then its value for a specific patient can be 38oC.

glossary.definition: a set of all features characterising a data object.
glossary.sentence: A feature set – a set of all features characterising a data object, for example, the patient's temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, and others can represent one patient in the dataset.

glossary.definition: a mathematical representation of relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm. Certain configuration variables describe it (see "A parameter" below).
glossary.sentence: A model - a mathematical representation of relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm. Certain configuration variables describe it (see "A parameter" below).

glossary.definition: a result of applying a model to given data objects.
glossary.sentence: A model output - a result of applying a model to given data objects.

glossary.definition: a subset of the dataset that is used in the testing of the model.
glossary.sentence: A test set – a subset of the dataset that is used in the testing of the model.

glossary.definition: the process of checking the performance of the model, usually using data previously unseen by the machine learning algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Testing – the process of checking the performance of the model, usually using data previously unseen by the machine learning algorithm.

glossary.definition: the process of determining the best values of parameters comprising a model and identifying relationships and patterns in the data.
glossary.sentence: Training - the process of determining the best values of parameters comprising a model and identifying relationships and patterns in the data.

glossary.definition: a subset of the dataset used in the training of the model.
glossary.sentence: A training set - a subset of the dataset used in the training of the model.

glossary.definition: a task performed when labels of data objects are presented as categories like spam or not spam, cat or dog, etc. Therefore, each data object belongs to a specific category/class.
glossary.sentence: Classification is performed when labels of data objects are presented as categories like spam or not spam, cat or dog, etc. Therefore, each data object belongs to a specific category/class.

glossary.definition: Carried out when labels of data objects are presented as real values (continuous) such as the price of a house or the number of graduating students.
glossary.sentence: Regression is carried out when labels of data objects are presented as real values (so they are continuous) as the price of a house, the number of graduating students, etc.

glossary.definition: Segmenting data into representative classes called clusters.
glossary.sentence: Clustering deals with segmenting data into representative classes called clusters (Jones, 2008; Pant, 2019).

glossary.definition: Finds rules based on common co-occurring values of data objects.
glossary.sentence: Association analysis finds rules that are based on common co-occurring values of data objects (Pant, 2019)

glossary.definition: Finding the most essential features to reduce the original feature set, especially by dropping less important features or merging correlated features.
glossary.sentence: Dimensionality reduction is associated with finding the most essential features to reduce the original feature set, especially by dropping less important features or merging correlated features (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019)

glossary.definition: Feedback received by an agent when it acts correctly, represented as a numerical value given with some delay.
glossary.sentence: The agent receives feedback: a reward if it acts correctly and a penalty if it acts incorrectly (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019).

glossary.definition: Feedback received by an agent when it acts incorrectly.
glossary.sentence: The agent receives feedback: a reward if it acts correctly and a penalty if it acts incorrectly (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019).

glossary.definition: Data processing that leads to a specific output: in classification task – a class label, in regression task - a prediction of the value.
glossary.sentence: a decision process: the data processing that leads to a specific output: in a classification task – a class label, in a regression task - a prediction of the value of a specified property of a new data object

glossary.definition: Measuring how good the output is by comparing it to known examples.
glossary.sentence: an error function: measuring how good the output is by comparing it to known examples

glossary.definition: Reducing the classification or prediction error by updating variables of the mathematical model used by the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: an updating or optimisation process: reducing the classification or prediction error by updating variables of the mathematical model used by the algorithm

glossary.definition: Includes three components : a decision process, an error function and an updating or optimisation process.
glossary.sentence: According to (Tamir, 2020), a supervised machine learning algorithm includes three components

glossary.definition: A supervised learning algorithm performing the task of regression, assuming a linear relationship between input variables (values of data object features) and a single output variable (a prediction). It uses a linear function to learn the values of weights from a given training dataset.
glossary.sentence: It is a supervised learning algorithm performing the task of regression

glossary.definition: The difference between predicted output value and real output value, accumulated using mean square error.
glossary.sentence: it starts with random values for weights; it calculates the output predicted by the model for each data object; it calculates the cost function and checks if the lowest possible loss is acquired

glossary.definition: A function used to measure the difference between predicted values and actual values in a regression model.
glossary.sentence: it calculates the cost function and checks if the lowest possible loss is acquired; if the lowest possible loss is not acquired, it calculates new values for weights

glossary.definition: A type of linear regression with a unique solution because the weights of the model can be calculated using specific equations.
glossary.sentence: For univariate linear regression, there is a unique solution because the weights of the model can be calculated using the following equations

glossary.definition: A scenario in linear regression where calculating the weights is not a trivial task.
glossary.sentence: However, calculating the weights for the case of multiple input features is not a trivial task.

glossary.definition: A mathematical solution like the one used in univariate linear regression, but not typically used in practice due to computational challenges.
glossary.sentence: There is also a closed-form solution like this one: However, this solution is not used in practice because the computation of matrices can fail if there is a problem of matrix singularity.

glossary.definition: A problem that can cause the computation of matrices to fail, making closed-form solutions impractical.
glossary.sentence: However, this solution is not used in practice because the computation of matrices can fail if there is a problem of matrix singularity.

glossary.definition: The high computational cost associated with inverting a multiplication of a transpose of vector X and vector X.
glossary.sentence: Moreover, the computational complexity of inverting a multiplication of a transpose of vector X and vector X is very high

glossary.definition: An optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, usually, another mechanism is used, which is called gradient descent. The gradient descent is an optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values.

glossary.definition: A mathematical concept used in gradient descent to calculate changes with respect to all independent variables.
glossary.sentence: The gradient descent is based on calculating a partial derivative with respect to all independent variables.

glossary.definition: The value of the cost function across the whole dataset, which is minimised according to changes in the values of the weights of the linear function.
glossary.sentence: The gradient descent technique thus allows the cost function value to be minimised according to changes in the values of the weights of the linear function.

glossary.definition: A parameter called learning rate used to apply the gradient descent, representing the rate at which Linear regression updates the weights of the linear function.
glossary.sentence: To apply the gradient descent, a hyperparameter α called learning rate is used. It represents a rate at which Linear regression updates the weights of the linear function.

glossary.definition: A hyperparameter representing the rate at which Linear regression updates the weights of the linear function, affecting the algorithm’s speed of learning and convergence to the error minima.
glossary.sentence: To apply the gradient descent, a hyperparameter α called learning rate is used. It represents a rate at which Linear regression updates the weights of the linear function.

glossary.definition: A metric used to evaluate the performance of a regression algorithm by accumulating the differences in the distance between the predicted output value and the real output value.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm's performance is evaluated using the mean square error that accumulates the differences in the distance between the predicted output value and the real output value.

glossary.definition: A machine learning task where the goal is to attribute a data object to a specific class/category or predict a discrete value.
glossary.sentence: In a classification task , it is necessary to attribute a data object to a specific class/category or, in other words, to predict a discrete value.

glossary.definition: The simplest case of classification where data are classified into two classes or the output variable can take either of two possible values.
glossary.sentence: The simplest case of classification is the so-called binary classification when data are classified in two classes or, in other words, the output variable can take either of two possible values

glossary.definition: Labels used in binary classification to represent two possible values.
glossary.sentence: In this case, usually, two class labels (1 and 0) are used.

glossary.definition: A type of classification where there are more than two classes/categories.
glossary.sentence: There are more than two classes/categories in multiclass classification ; for example, in animal classification, we can use labels like Cat, Dog, Horse, etc. ; we can classify movies according to their genres like Romance, Comedy, Horror, Drama, etc.

glossary.definition: A supervised machine learning algorithm used for the classification task, especially binary classification.
glossary.sentence: Logistic regression , in its turn, is used for the classification task, especially a binary classification task.

glossary.definition: The output of logistic regression, which predicts one of two classes/categories to which a data object belongs.
glossary.sentence: Thus, the linear regression obtains as input data the values of the features of data objects and outputs the real number as its result. For example, based on data about the house area, number of floors, and land area under the house, it predicts the house price. In turn, logistic regression also receives as input data the values of the features of data objects, but outputs a binary class label of this object as a result.

glossary.definition: A mathematical function used in logistic regression to squash the value of the linear function within the range of [0,1].
glossary.sentence: Logistic regression also tries finding a mathematical function that relates x to y . It also uses the linear function as the linear regression does. Still, after receiving the linear function's value, it applies a sigmoid function to squash the value of teh linear function within the range of [0,1] using the sigmoid function.

glossary.definition: A value of 0.5 used in logistic regression to decide about a class/category of a data object based on the squashed value from the sigmoid function.
glossary.sentence: The threshold value of 0.5 at which the function crosses the y axis is used to decide about a class/category of a data object.

glossary.definition: Building a hierarchy of clusters characterized by merging/dividing clusters at each level; lowest level contains single data objects, highest level contains all data objects.
glossary.sentence: Hierarchical clustering is about building a hierarchy of clusters that is characterised by the following aspects (Hastie, 2017) : the clusters at each level of the hierarchy are created by merging/dividing clusters at the next lower level; at the lowest level, each cluster contains a single data object; at the highest level, there is only one cluster containing all of the data objects.

glossary.definition: Bottom-up approach where each data object starts in its own cluster and pairs of clusters are recursively merged based on smallest intergroup dissimilarity.
glossary.sentence: Agglomerative hierarchical clustering is a bottom-up approach to building a hierarchy of clusters. Therefore, each data object initially is attributed to its own cluster. Then, at each level, a selected pair of clusters is recursively merged into a single cluster. This produces a grouping at the next higher level with one less cluster.

glossary.definition: Top-down approach starting with all data objects in one cluster and recursively splitting clusters based on largest between-group dissimilarity.
glossary.sentence: Divisive hierarchical clustering applies a top-down clustering approach. Therefore, it starts with a cluster with all data objects inside it. Then, at each hierarchy level, it recursively splits one of the existing clusters into two new clusters.

glossary.definition: Methods to measure similarity between clusters for merging rules.
glossary.sentence: There are several methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters, and they are often called linkage methods .

glossary.definition: Cluster similarity based on distance between most distant elements of two clusters.
glossary.sentence: Complete-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: Cluster similarity based on distance between closest elements of two clusters.
glossary.sentence: Single-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: Cluster similarity based on average distance between each data object in one cluster and every data object in another cluster.
glossary.sentence: Average-linkage: the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster: where G, H – clusters; NG – the number of data objects in the cluster G; NH – the number of data objects in the cluster H; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i’ – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: Tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in a dataset, showing how clusters were formed.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram is an output of hierarchical clustering. It is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset. According to (Hastie, 2017) , “a dendrogram provides a highly interpretable complete description of the hierarchical clustering in a graphical format” . Thus, it provides insight into the way how clusters were formed.

glossary.definition: Representation of merging data objects into clusters within a dendrogram; each has a height indicating similarity.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram consists of (Figure 5): clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height; leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.

glossary.definition: Terminal points of clades in a dendrogram, representing individual data objects used in the clustering process.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram consists of (Figure 5): clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height; leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.

glossary.definition: Line drawn through a dendrogram to determine the number of clusters based on intersections with vertical lines.
glossary.sentence: A horizontal cut- off line is usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm. The number of vertical lines intersected by the horizontal cut- off line represents the number of clusters.

glossary.definition: Evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects. It is calculated by summing up values of the main diagonal of the confusion matrix and dividing it by the total number of data objects in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: Accuracy metric evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects

glossary.definition: A table used to calculate accuracy for multiclass classification by summing up values of the main diagonal.
glossary.sentence: Accuracy for the multiclass classification is calculated by summing up values of the main diagonal of the confusion matrix and dividing it by the total number of data objects in the dataset

glossary.definition: Evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives. It is the probability that the classifier is right when labelling an example as positive.
glossary.sentence: A precision metric evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives. In other words, the accuracy of the positive predictions

glossary.definition: Evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects. It is the probability that a positive data object will be correctly recognised by the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: A recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects

glossary.definition: Represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall. It estimates the number of data objects classified correctly.
glossary.sentence: The F1 score is one more metric used in the classification tasks. It represents the harmonic mean of precision and recall because the classifier will only get a high F1 score if both recall and precision are high

glossary.definition: Plots false positive rate (x-axis) versus true positive rate (y-axis) for each value of the hyperparameter, helping visualise values that would be good choices for the tradeoff.
glossary.sentence: The behaviour of the classifier under different values of its parameters can be visualised by the so-called ROC (The receiver operating characteristic) curve

glossary.definition: Provides a single-number summary of the ROC curve. It measures the entire two-dimensional area underneath the entire ROC curve.
glossary.sentence: Area under the ROC Curve (AUC) allows comparing different classifiers. It provides a single-number summary of the ROC curve

glossary.definition: It represents the closeness of each data object in a cluster to data objects in the neighbouring clusters. The values lie in a range [-1, 1]. Closer values to +1 indicate better separated clusters.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, it represents the closeness of each data object in a cluster to data objects in the neighbouring clusters. The values of the Silhouette score lies in a range [-1, 1].

glossary.definition: Demands dividing data objects into representative groups. Its output is groups of data objects.
glossary.sentence: In general, the clustering task demands dividing data objects into representative groups. Thus, its output is groups of data objects

glossary.definition: Increasing precision reduces recall and vice versa.
glossary.sentence: Unfortunately, you can't have it both ways: increasing precision reduces recall and vice versa. This is called the precision/recall tradeoff

glossary.definition: A patient suffering from X illness but not diagnosed as such.
glossary.sentence: Kubat (2017) gives an example of a medical diagnosis, in which a patient suffering from X illness but not diagnosed as such represents a false negative

glossary.definition: A plot representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis.
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Scores are usually plotted on the so-called Silhouette Plot , representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis (Fig.6).

glossary.definition: A cell in the human brain with a cell body containing a nucleus and root-like extensions: dendrites and an axon.
glossary.sentence: The human brain consists of cells called neurons. Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon (Fig.

glossary.definition: Root-like extensions that serve for receiving signals from other neurons.
glossary.sentence: Dendrites look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons.

glossary.definition: A root-like extension that splits into branches, each terminating in an endbulb close to the dendrites of another neuron.
glossary.sentence: The axon itself eventually splits into a number of branches, each terminating in a little endbulb that comes close to the dendrites of another neuron.

glossary.definition: The site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or the cell body of another neuron.
glossary.sentence: A sending neuron transmits a signal along its axon to a synapse, which is the site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or the cell body of another neuron.

glossary.definition: Signals that promote firing.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively.

glossary.definition: Signals that inhibit firing.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively.

glossary.definition: A mathematical model of a neuron defined by McCulloch and Pitts in 1943.
glossary.sentence: In 1943, McCulloch and Pitts defined a mathematical model of the neuron (Ertel, 2011; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011), which laid fundamentals for artificial neural networks.

glossary.definition: Provides the offset of the line/hyperplane from the origin.
glossary.sentence: a bias input which is also commonly applied, and it provides the offset of the line/hyperplane from the origin.

glossary.definition: Determines the output of the neuron and allows the neural network to model complex non-linear relationships.
glossary.sentence: a transfer (activation) function that determines the output of the neuron and allows the neural network to model complex non-linear relationships;

glossary.definition: a neural network architecture that learns based on assigning initial random values to weights, selecting training examples, performing feed-forwarding and backpropagation, and continuing the process until a termination criterion is achieved.
glossary.sentence: The learning in multilayer perceptron is based on the following steps (Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011): assigning initial random values to weights; selecting a training example from the training set; performing feed-forwarding – propagation of inputs forward in the network; performing backpropagation of error backwards in the network: from the output layer to the previous hidden layer, and from this hidden layer back to a hidden layer before, etc. until reaching the weights of the input layer; the training process continues until some termination criterion is achieved (see above). Therefore, the learning in the multilayer perceptron is based on the backpropagation algorithm .

glossary.definition: an algorithm used for learning in multilayer perceptrons that includes two main phases: feed-forwarding and backpropagation, and allows modifying weights in all network layers through the propagation of the error from the output layer to the input layer.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the learning in the multilayer perceptron is based on the backpropagation algorithm . It includes two main phases: feed-forwarding and backpropagation (Fig. The inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the output layer during the feed-forwarding .

glossary.definition: the phase of the backpropagation algorithm where inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the output layer.
glossary.sentence: The inputs are propagated over the network from the input layer to the output layer during the feed-forwarding .

glossary.definition: a type of machine learning where the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels, meaning that feeding a particular data object into the network should produce a known output.
glossary.sentence: In a supervised machine learning task, the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels. It means that by feeding a particular data object into the network, we expect to acquire a known output.

glossary.definition: the difference between the predicted output and the actual output in a supervised machine learning task.
glossary.sentence: However, in reality, the output predicted by the network differs from the actual output. Thus, there is a difference between the right output and the predicted output. It is called an error.

glossary.definition: units in a neural network that do not have direct access to the input or output data but are responsible for some fraction of the error in each of the output units.
glossary.sentence: The training data do not say what value the hidden processing units should have, and each hidden processing unit is responsible for some fraction of the error in each of the output units (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: the cumulative error contributed by the weights of all layers in a neural network.
glossary.sentence: As a result, the weights of all layers contribute to the total error.

glossary.definition: neurons in the output layer of a neural network for which the error can be calculated using the predicted output and the real class label.
glossary.sentence: In the output neuron, we know the predicted output and the real class label and, thus, can calculate the error in the following way:

glossary.definition: neurons in the hidden layers of a neural network for which the error is calculated based on the errors of the neurons in the subsequent layer.
glossary.sentence: In the hidden layers, the error for each processing unit is calculated in the following way:

glossary.definition: intelligent computer systems that contain two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine.
glossary.sentence: In the field of artificial intelligence, knowledge representation is needed to develop knowledge-based intelligent computer systems. A knowledge-based intelligent system is an intelligent computer system that contains two main components – the knowledge base and the inference engine (Hopgood, 2001).

glossary.definition: A structure that preserves the form of rules used by human experts to determine conditions for successful problem-solving.
glossary.sentence: the structure of rules used by the human experts must be preserved in building a knowledge base

glossary.definition: The processing mechanism of a rule-based expert system that handles the knowledge stored in the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge is stored in the knowledge base, but its processing mechanism is the inference engine

glossary.definition: stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base differs from a database. The database stores unrelated facts, for example, purchase transactions or grades of students. The knowledge base stores a representation not only of facts but also of relations among them.

glossary.definition: a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain.
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base. Such systems can offer a variety of user interfaces, for example, they could offer creation of diagrams representing some scenarios from the problem domain or the creation of structured description of knowledge.

glossary.definition: a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts.
glossary.sentence: Intelligent computer systems that emulate the human expert’s methodology and performance are called expert systems (Luger, 2009). An expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation schemas are means that offer a set of formal conventions and rules used to describe knowledge. Using them, a knowledge representation or description is created.

glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Several categories of knowledge representation schemas exist in the field of artificial intelligence (Grimm et al., 2007; Mylopoulos and Levesque, 1983): logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge;

glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them.
glossary.sentence: networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them;

glossary.definition: Schemas that use graphs to represent knowledge in a structured way, similar to semantic networks.
glossary.sentence: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge.

glossary.term: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas
glossary.definition: represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.
glossary.sentence: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: theories of human mind that were developed in psychology. These theories postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts.
glossary.sentence: Networked knowledge representation schemas are based on associationist theories of human mind that were developed in psychology. These theories postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts (Luger, 2009), for example, the concept “university” for the most part of students could be associated with such concepts as lectures, students, professors, exams, science etc.

glossary.definition: The average time it takes for people to respond to a question.
glossary.sentence: So, the previously described experiment showed that the mean reaction time to the question “Can a canary bird fly?” is higher than the mean reaction time to the question “Can a canary bird sing?”

glossary.definition: A way of organizing knowledge where general features are associated with broader concepts and specific features with narrower concepts.
glossary.sentence: people do not store all features of a specific bird, but they organize knowledge about birds in a hierarchical manner: general features characterizing all birds are associated with a concept “Bird”

glossary.definition: The sequence of steps or levels one must navigate through a hierarchical structure to find information.
glossary.sentence: as a result, to find an answer people need to move along the hierarchical path in the structure of the memory.

glossary.definition: Knowledge that is stored at the level of a concept it belongs to rather than being derived from broader concepts.
glossary.sentence: The researchers also have revealed that exceptional knowledge is stored at the level of a concept it belongs to.

glossary.definition: A knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph where nodes correspond to domain objects or concepts and directed labeled arcs represent relations between them.
glossary.sentence: Based on the made experiment, Collins and Quillian created a semantic network that is displayed in Figure 4. (the text fragment is based on information given in (Luger, 2009)) A semantic network is a knowledge representation schema that represents knowledge as a graph: labelled nodes in this graph correspond to domain objects/concepts but directed labelled arcs – to relations between them

glossary.definition: A hierarchical relationship between narrower concepts (subclasses) and broader concepts (classes), represented by titles such as “is a” or “is a subclass of.”
glossary.sentence: A relation “subclass-class” (also “subset-set”, “subtype-type”, “ subkind -kind”) is represented by a title of “is a” (also “is a subclass of”, “is a subset of”, “is a type of” or “is a kind of”).

glossary.definition: A hierarchical relationship between specific objects and the classes they belong to, represented by titles such as “is an instance/example of.”
glossary.sentence: A relation “instance-class/set/type/kind” is represented by the title of “is an instance/example of”.

glossary.definition: A relationship between parts of an object and the whole object, represented by the title “Part of.”
glossary.sentence: A relation “part-whole” is represented by the title of “Part of”.

glossary.definition: A relationship between objects or concepts and their properties, represented by titles such as “property/has a property of.”
glossary.sentence: A relation “object-property” is represented by the title of “property/has a property of”.

glossary.definition: A relationship between properties and their values, represented by the title “value.”
glossary.sentence: A relation “property-value” is represented by the title of “value”.

glossary.definition: Relations that specify any other kind of relationships not covered by predefined types, such as ‘like,’ ‘own,’ or ‘travel.’
glossary.sentence: Linguistic relations (Fig.11). They allow specifying any other kind of relations (for example, like, own, travel, etc.) that cannot be represented by the previously mentioned types of relations.

glossary.definition: A feature of semantic networks where narrower nodes automatically acquire knowledge from broader nodes in the hierarchy.
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is a process by which a narrower node automatically inherits knowledge from the network, that is, a subclass/subset/subtype/ subkind node inherits knowledge from a class/set/type/kind node or an instance inherits knowledge from a subclass/subset/subtype/ subkind node or a class/set/type/kind node.

glossary.definition: A self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system. It can be used for various purposes like generating an image of an object, calculating numerical values (e.g., taxes), or acquiring data from external sources.
glossary.sentence: A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system

glossary.definition: A procedure that is invoked as a side effect of some other action in the knowledge base. It controls actions for slot-level manipulations and is triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed.
glossary.sentence: Demons implement actions in a frame-based expert system

glossary.definition: A type of expert system where knowledge is organized into frames, and demons are used to implement actions within these frames.
glossary.sentence: Demons implement actions in a frame-based expert system

glossary.definition: A specific piece of data or information stored within a slot in a frame. It can be accessed, changed, added, or removed, triggering associated demons.
glossary.sentence: Demons are triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed

glossary.definition: A type of demon that is triggered when the slot value is unspecified and needs to be calculated or set.
glossary.sentence: The common demons are: IF-NEEDED – it is triggered when the slot value is unspecified, and it is necessary to calculate/to set it;

glossary.definition: A type of demon that is triggered when a slot value changes.
glossary.sentence: IF-CHANGED – it is triggered when a slot value changes;

glossary.definition: A type of demon that is triggered when a slot value is unspecified and added to the frame.
glossary.sentence: IF-ADDED – it is triggered when a slot value is unspecified, and it is added to the frame;

glossary.definition: A type of demon that is triggered when a slot value is deleted.
glossary.sentence: IF-REMOVED – it is triggered when a slot value is deleted.

glossary.definition: A high-level frame that defines general attributes and demons which can be inherited by subclass frames.
glossary.sentence: There are standard demons defined for frames, although different shells for creating frame-based knowledge bases can offer a slightly different set of demons with different titles.

glossary.definition: A frame that inherits attributes and demons from a class frame and may have additional slots or specific modifications.
glossary.sentence: Demons are usually defined in class and sub-class frames and then inherited and executed in instance frames.

glossary.definition: A specific example of a class or subclass frame that contains actual data or values for the slots defined in its parent frames.
glossary.sentence: Demons are usually defined in class and sub-class frames and then inherited and executed in instance frames.

glossary.definition: Frames that incorporate demons, allowing for procedural actions to be associated with the data stored within them.
glossary.sentence: Object-oriented frames are acquired when demons are assigned to frames.

glossary.definition: A type of intelligent computer system that combines data-driven and goal-driven search directions to maximize the use of new information in problem-solving.
glossary.sentence: It is possible to combine both search directions in a rule-based expert system ( Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: A rule that suggests a specific sequence of things to try and controls search, making the system behave logically.
glossary.sentence: when a mechanic says, “if the engine does not turn over and the lights don’t come on, then check the battery” , he/she suggests a specific sequence of things to try

glossary.definition: A type of intelligent system where the THEN part of rules contains statements, allowing for logical deductions to be made.
glossary.sentence: If statements are added to the THEN part of rules, then a deductive intelligent system is acquired;

glossary.definition: A type of intelligent system where the THEN part of rules contains actions, allowing for immediate responses based on conditions.
glossary.sentence: If the THEN part of rules contains actions, then a reactive intelligent system is acquired.

glossary.definition: An inference engine chosen when a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred.
glossary.sentence: If a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred, choose the forward chaining inference engine

glossary.definition: An inference engine chosen when a domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and then attempts to find facts to prove it.
glossary.sentence: if your domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and then attempts to find facts to prove it, choose the backward chaining inference engine

glossary.definition: The method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set

glossary.definition: The set of enabled rules from which one rule is chosen to fire during conflict resolution.
glossary.sentence: from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set

glossary.definition: Explicitly stated values assigned to rules to determine which rule to fire when more than one rule can fire.
glossary.sentence: Rules can be written so that each has an explicitly stated priority value, and, thus, if more than one rule can fire, the one with the highest priority is chosen

glossary.definition: A method of conflict resolution based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one.
glossary.sentence: This method is also known as the longest matching strategy: it is based on the assumption that a specific rule processes more information than a general one

glossary.definition: Tags attached to each fact in the working memory to determine which rule to fire based on the most recently entered data.
glossary.sentence: This method relies on time tags attached to each fact in the working memory; thus, in the conflict set, the expert system first fires the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory

glossary.definition: Mechanisms used by rule-based expert systems to represent and reason with incomplete and uncertain knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors

glossary.definition: The control cycle in which the selected rule can be displayed at each stage.
glossary.sentence: At each stage of the control cycle "Recognize-act", the selected rule can be displayed
